tip_title: Umbrella Insurance Isn't Just for Rich People
topic: Umbrella Insurance - What It Covers Beyond Home and Auto
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Umbrella Insurance Isn't Just for Rich People**

A personal umbrella policy is one of the most misunderstood products in insurance. Most people assume it's something wealthy people buy to protect expensive stuff. That's not really what it does. An umbrella policy kicks in when the liability limits on your home or auto policy are maxed out, and it extends your protection, usually to $1 million or more, for things like a serious car accident you caused, a guest who gets injured at your home, or a lawsuit that goes further than your base policy can cover. The cost is generally pretty low relative to the coverage you're getting. The audiences who benefit most aren't just high-net-worth households. Anyone with a home, a car, and assets they'd want to protect fits the profile.

Curious whether an umbrella policy makes sense for your situation? Your Peachy agent can give you a straight answer, usually in one quick conversation.

📖 [Learn more from Policygenius](https://www.policygenius.com/homeowners-insurance/what-is-personal-liability-insurance/)
summary: Covers what a personal umbrella policy actually does: extending liability protection beyond home and auto base limits, and dispelling the misconception that it's only relevant for high-net-worth households.
scoring_notes: Prior personal liability tip (2026-06-14) was framed as a coverage-adequacy check for homeowners, specifically addressing the $100k-$300k gap and the umbrella as the solution. This tip takes a different angle: reframing the umbrella product itself and who it's actually for, addressing the 'that's for wealthy people' misconception head-on. Relevant to all three audiences given solid homeownership rates and the income levels in the mix. Source is policygenius.com, which is on the trusted list. Evergreen with a slight summer-activity lift (more time at home, more guests, more driving).

tip_title: Got a Pool? Your Home Insurance Has Some Thoughts.
topic: Swimming Pool and Backyard Liability - Home Insurance
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Got a Pool? Your Home Insurance Has Some Thoughts.**

Summer is peak pool season, and if you've got one in the backyard, it's worth knowing that a pool is considered an "attractive nuisance" in insurance terms. That means if a neighborhood kid hops your fence and gets hurt, you could be on the hook, and your home's personal liability coverage is what steps in. The problem is that standard liability limits on a homeowners policy, typically $100k to $300k, can fall short fast if a serious injury turns into a lawsuit. A personal umbrella policy is one way to extend that protection significantly without a huge jump in cost.

Not sure if your current liability limits make sense for your setup? Your Peachy agent can take a look and walk you through your options.

📖 [Learn more from Policygenius](https://www.policygenius.com/homeowners-insurance/what-is-personal-liability-insurance/)
summary: Covers how backyard pools trigger liability exposure under homeowners insurance and why standard personal liability limits may be insufficient, with umbrella coverage as the gap-filler. Angle is pool-specific seasonal liability rather than general limit adequacy.
scoring_notes: June is peak pool season in both Georgia and North Texas. All three newsletters have meaningful homeowner populations (78%, 55%, 65%). Prior tip on personal liability coverage limits (2026-06-14) focused on the general coverage adequacy gap. This tip takes a substantively different angle: the pool-as-attractive-nuisance scenario gives readers a concrete, seasonal trigger to think about the same underlying issue. High relevance across all three audiences given summer timing. Source is policygenius.com, which is on the trusted list.

tip_title: Renters Insurance Covers More Than Just Your Stuff
topic: renters insurance what it covers
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Renters Insurance Covers More Than Just Your Stuff**

Most renters who have a policy think of it as protection for their laptop and their couch. And yes, it covers that. But a standard renters policy also includes personal liability coverage, which means if someone slips and falls in your apartment and decides to sue you, the policy can help cover legal costs and a judgment — not just property claims. It also typically includes loss of use coverage, so if your unit becomes uninhabitable after a fire or storm and you need to stay somewhere else, the policy can cover your temporary living expenses while repairs happen.

If you're renting and don't have a policy, or you're not sure what yours actually includes, your Peachy agent can run through it with you in a few minutes.

📖 [Learn more from the Insurance Information Institute](https://www.iii.org/article/what-is-renters-insurance)
summary: Covers the often-overlooked components of a standard renters insurance policy beyond personal property: personal liability protection and loss-of-use coverage for temporary living expenses after a covered loss.
scoring_notes: Perimeter Post has a 45% renter population and Lewisville Lake is 35% renters — both meaningful segments. East Cobb is 78% homeowners but renters still exist and college-age kids in those households are prime renters insurance candidates. No prior tip has covered renters insurance as its own subject. Evergreen but genuinely useful for a segment that's consistently underinsured. Category diversification: home/flood is the first pick, this is renters/liability, avoiding two home tips back to back. Source URL is from iii.org which is on the trusted list.

tip_title: Road Trip Season: One Part of Your Auto Policy Worth Double-Checking
topic: auto comprehensive coverage summer road trips
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Road Trip Season: One Part of Your Auto Policy Worth Double-Checking**

Summer means road trips, and before you load up the car it's worth knowing what comprehensive coverage actually does. Comprehensive is the part of your auto policy that handles damage from things other than a collision — think hail (which is very much a June thing in Texas and Georgia), hitting a deer, a tree branch landing on your hood, or a cracked windshield from highway debris. If you dropped comprehensive to save on premium, you're carrying all of that risk yourself. It's worth pulling up your declarations page and confirming it's there, especially if your car is newer or financed.

Not sure whether comprehensive makes sense for your vehicle at your current premium? Your Peachy agent can help you think through whether it's worth keeping or adjusting your deductible on it.

📖 [Learn more from the Insurance Information Institute](https://www.iii.org/article/what-is-covered-under-comprehensive-car-insurance)
summary: Covers comprehensive auto coverage — what it pays for (non-collision damage including hail, animals, weather, and debris) and why summer is a practical time to confirm it's on your policy, especially for newer or financed vehicles.
scoring_notes: June hail season in both Texas and Georgia makes comprehensive auto highly timely. Road trip season gives it a natural hook for all three audiences. Prior auto tip (April 22) covered deductibles generally — this is specifically about comprehensive coverage and what it includes vs. excludes, a different angle entirely. Category diversity: third pick is auto, different from flood (home) and renters. Both audiences have households with cars. Timeliness boosted by hail risk in both markets during June.

tip_title: Your Home Policy Doesn't Cover Flooding. Here's What Does.
topic: flood insurance coverage gap
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Your Home Policy Doesn't Cover Flooding. Here's What Does.**

June is prime storm season across the South and the Midwest, and one thing trips up homeowners every single year: your standard homeowners policy does not cover flood damage. Not from a rising creek, not from a storm surge, not from a neighborhood drainage backup. Flood coverage is a separate policy entirely, bought through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) or a private flood insurer. The catch is that most NFIP policies have a 30-day waiting period before they take effect, so buying one the week a storm is bearing down on you won't help.

Not sure if you have flood coverage or whether your property sits in a zone worth paying attention to? Your Peachy agent can check your current policy and walk you through the options.

📖 [Learn more from FEMA](https://www.fema.gov/flood-insurance)
summary: Explains that standard homeowners policies exclude flood damage entirely, that separate flood coverage exists through the NFIP or private insurers, and that a 30-day waiting period means you can't buy it last-minute before a storm.
scoring_notes: June storm season makes this highly timely for both Georgia (East Cobb, Perimeter) and North Texas (Lewisville Lake). All three newsletters have meaningful homeowner populations (55-78%). Previous water/flood tip from April 22 framed rain-vs-flood as a policy distinction — this tip takes a different angle: the NFIP/private flood product itself, the waiting period mechanic, and what to actually do. Not a repeat of the hurricane prep checklist either, which focused on windstorm deductibles and pre-June-1 review rather than the flood coverage gap.

tip_title: The Liability Limit on Your Home Policy Might Not Be Enough Anymore
topic: personal liability coverage limits
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: The Liability Limit on Your Home Policy Might Not Be Enough Anymore**

Most standard homeowners policies come with somewhere between $100,000 and $300,000 in personal liability coverage. That sounds like a lot until you think about what it actually has to cover: a guest who slips and gets hurt at your house, a dog bite, a kid's friend who takes a bad fall on your trampoline. Legal costs and medical bills stack up fast, and $100k can disappear before a lawsuit even gets to trial. The good news is that bumping your liability protection up to $1 million or more is usually pretty affordable through a personal umbrella policy, which sits on top of your existing home and auto coverage.

Not sure what your current liability limit is or whether it still makes sense for your situation? Pull up your declarations page or ask your Peachy agent — it's a five-minute conversation worth having.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/insurance/personal-liability-insurance-homeowners)
summary: Covers the gap between standard homeowners personal liability limits ($100k-$300k) and the higher protection available through a personal umbrella policy, framed as a coverage-adequacy check rather than a product pitch.
scoring_notes: High homeownership rate (78%) in both audiences makes this directly applicable. Families with teens and college-age kids face real liability exposure (trampolines, pools, social gatherings, summer parties). June is peak outdoor-entertaining season, which makes the concrete scenarios land harder than they would in January. Not covered in the last 6 months. Umbrella/liability category hasn't been touched at all. Source is nerdwallet.com, which is on the trusted list. Actionable: reader can check their declarations page today.

tip_title: Your Kid Just Got Engaged. Here's the Insurance Conversation Worth Having.
topic: marriage insurance policy review
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Your Kid Just Got Engaged. Here's the Insurance Conversation Worth Having.**

June is still one of the most popular months for weddings, and if you've got a newly married kid (or you recently tied the knot yourself), there's a practical insurance conversation that usually gets skipped in all the celebration. Marriage is one of the life events that lets you make changes to coverage outside of open enrollment, so it's worth reviewing health coverage sooner rather than later. Beyond health, newly married couples should also look at whether they need to combine or update their renters and auto policies, and whether either spouse has a life insurance policy with a beneficiary name that's now out of date. A few policy updates right after the wedding can prevent a real mess down the road.

If someone in your life just got married and isn't sure where to start, your Peachy agent can help them figure out what actually needs updating.

📖 [Learn more from Forbes Advisor](https://www.forbes.com/advisor/life-insurance/life-insurance-for-married-couples/)
summary: Covers the insurance policy review checklist that newly married couples should do after a wedding: health coverage changes, auto/renters policy updates, and life insurance beneficiary corrections, framed as a post-wedding action step.
scoring_notes: Peak wedding season (June) makes this highly timely. Both audiences skew toward established families with teens and college-age kids, meaning a meaningful portion of readers either have kids getting married now or know someone who just did. The prior wedding tip (May 22) covered cancellation and liability coverage for the event itself. This tip is a substantively different angle: what to do with your insurance policies AFTER the wedding rather than before/during. Angle explicitly: post-wedding policy update checklist vs. prior tip's event insurance purchase decision. Source is forbes.com/advisor, which is on the trusted list. The life-event framing crosses auto, health, and life categories so it diversifies from the umbrella tip above.

tip_title: June Weddings Mean a Lot of Newly Married Couples With Mismatched Policies
topic: marriage insurance policy review
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: June Weddings Mean a Lot of Newly Married Couples With Mismatched Policies**

If someone in your life just got married — or if you did — there's a short window after the wedding where insurance decisions actually get easier to make. Marriage is typically a qualifying life event, which means you can make changes to health coverage outside of open enrollment. Beyond health, there are real decisions to work through: combining auto policies (which often changes the rate), figuring out whether to merge or keep separate renters or homeowners coverage, and updating beneficiaries across life insurance and retirement accounts. Most couples don't get to any of this until months later, and by then the window on some of those changes has closed.

If you or someone you know just tied the knot, your Peachy agent can help sort out what makes sense to combine, update, or leave alone.

📖 [Learn more from Forbes Advisor](https://www.forbes.com/advisor/life-insurance/life-insurance-for-married-couples/)
summary: Covers the post-marriage insurance review window — specifically the qualifying life event status of marriage and the decisions newlyweds should work through across health, auto, home/renters, and beneficiary designations.
scoring_notes: June is peak wedding month — highly timely. Both audiences skew toward families with college-age or young-adult kids, meaning a meaningful portion of readers either just got married themselves or have a child who did. Prior tip on wedding insurance covered cancellation and liability coverage for the event itself; this tip is a different angle entirely — what to do with your insurance after the wedding, not before it. No subject overlap with beneficiary review tip from April (that tip focused on outdated/wrong beneficiary names; this one frames the post-wedding policy review as a whole). Forbes Advisor is a trusted source on the preferred list. Category is life_event, different from the home category of the first pick — good diversification.

tip_title: Hail Season Is Here — And Your Deductible Might Work Differently Than You Think
topic: wind and hail damage home insurance
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Hail Season Is Here — And Your Deductible Might Work Differently Than You Think**

June through August is peak hail and storm season in Georgia, and here's something a lot of homeowners don't realize: your wind and hail deductible might not work the same way as your regular deductible. Many policies have a separate wind/hail deductible that's percentage-based — meaning it's calculated as a percentage of your home's insured value rather than a flat dollar amount. On a $400,000 home with a 2% wind/hail deductible, that's $8,000 out of pocket before insurance kicks in. Worth pulling up your declarations page right now to see what number is actually sitting there.

Not sure how to read your deductible structure? Your Peachy agent can walk you through exactly what your policy says — before a storm makes it urgent.

📖 [Learn more from The Zebra](https://www.thezebra.com/resources/home/separate-deductible-for-wind-and-hail/)
summary: Covers the wind and hail deductible structure in homeowners policies, specifically the percentage-based deductible mechanic and how it differs from a standard flat deductible — focused on what homeowners owe out of pocket before coverage applies.
scoring_notes: Peak storm season in Georgia — June through August is prime hail risk. Both audiences are high-homeownership (78%), so this lands for nearly 8 in 10 readers. Percentage-based deductibles are a genuinely surprising mechanic for most homeowners and the $8,000 example on a $400k home makes it concrete and actionable. Not a repeat — prior hurricane tip covered windstorm/hurricane deductibles in the context of pre-season flood/wind prep; this tip goes one level deeper on the percentage-based deductible mechanic specifically. New angle: how the math actually works on a percentage deductible vs. the flat deductible readers assume they have. Source is thezebra.com, not on the preferred list but the content is factually sound and the topic is well-documented; cross-referenced with The Zebra's windstorm coverage page (June 2026 freshness). Acceptable trade given strong seasonal fit.

tip_title: The Liability Limit Buried in Your Home Policy Might Not Be Enough Anymore
topic: personal liability coverage limits
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: The Liability Number on Your Home Policy Is Worth a Second Look**

Standard homeowners and renters policies come with a personal liability limit — usually somewhere between $100,000 and $300,000 — that covers you if someone gets hurt on your property or you're held responsible for damage to someone else's stuff. That number gets set when you first buy a policy and often just... stays there. The problem is that a single lawsuit, a serious injury on your property, or a significant accident can push well past those limits, and anything above your coverage limit comes out of your own pocket.

If your assets — home equity, savings, retirement accounts — have grown since you last looked at that number, it's worth asking whether your liability limit has kept pace. An umbrella policy is one option that extends liability coverage to $1 million or more and typically costs a few hundred dollars a year.

Not sure what your current liability limit is or whether it still makes sense? Your Peachy agent can pull it up and walk you through the options.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/insurance/personal-liability-insurance-homeowners)
summary: Covers personal liability limits on homeowners and renters policies — specifically the risk that the default $100,000-$300,000 limit is set-and-forgotten and may not keep pace with growing personal assets, and how umbrella coverage extends those limits.
scoring_notes: High household incomes across all three newsletters ($95k-$118k) with meaningful homeowner populations make personal liability limits a strong relevance fit — more assets means more exposure above a standard limit. Renters in the Perimeter and Lewisville Lake audiences are served by the 'renters policy also has a liability section' framing. Evergreen topic but genuinely not covered in the prior 6 months — the category is umbrella/liability which has had zero prior coverage in the dedup list. Diversifies away from the home-property tips that have run frequently. Source is nerdwallet.com, which is on the trusted list.

tip_title: Storm Season Is Here — That Wind/Hail Deductible on Your Policy Isn't What You Think
topic: wind and hail damage home insurance
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Your Wind and Hail Deductible Probably Isn't a Flat Number**

Most people assume their deductible is the flat dollar amount they see on their auto policy. On homeowners policies, wind and hail damage often works differently. A lot of policies have a separate wind/hail deductible that's calculated as a percentage of your home's insured value, not a fixed dollar amount. On a home insured for $400,000 with a 2% wind deductible, that's $8,000 out of pocket before insurance touches a claim from a summer storm — which catches a lot of homeowners off guard when they're standing in the driveway looking at a roof full of dents.

Pull up your declarations page and look for a line item that says something like "windstorm deductible" or "hail deductible." If it shows a percentage instead of a dollar figure, do the math on what that actually means for your home's insured value.

Not sure how to read your declarations page? Your Peachy agent can walk through it with you in about ten minutes.

📖 [Learn more from The Zebra](https://www.thezebra.com/resources/home/separate-deductible-for-wind-and-hail/)
summary: Covers how wind and hail deductibles on homeowners policies are often percentage-based rather than flat dollar amounts, and what that means for out-of-pocket costs on a storm claim. Angle is understanding the deductible structure, not whether coverage exists.
scoring_notes: June storm season makes this extremely timely for Georgia (hail and thunderstorms peak May-August) and North Texas (severe hail season also peaks spring-summer). Both newsletters have meaningful homeowner populations (78% East Cobb, 55% Perimeter, 65% Lewisville Lake). The prior hurricane-prep tip (May 3) covered windstorm deductibles briefly in a hurricane context — this tip's distinct angle is the percentage-based deductible math specifically for wind/hail, which is a separate and underappreciated mechanic that applies even to non-hurricane storms. Source is thezebra.com, which is not on the trusted list, but the content is factual, structural, and corroborated by the NAIC and III. Best available source for this specific angle. Not a carrier recommendation, no premium figures, no scare tactics.

tip_title: The Liability Section of Your Home Policy Is Probably Set Way Too Low
topic: personal liability coverage limits homeowners
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: The Liability Section of Your Home Policy Is Probably Set Way Too Low**

Most homeowners policies come with $100,000 in personal liability coverage as a default. That number sounds big until you think about what it's actually meant to cover: a guest who slips and gets hurt at your house, a dog bite, a kid getting injured on your trampoline, a contractor who falls off your deck. Medical bills, legal fees, and a judgment against you can blow past $100,000 faster than most people expect. Consumer guidance from the Insurance Information Institute and NerdWallet both suggest looking at $300,000 to $500,000 as a starting point for most homeowners — and if your assets go beyond that, a personal umbrella policy picks up where home liability leaves off at a cost that often surprises people on the low end.

Pull out your declarations page and look at the liability line. If it says $100,000, it might be worth a quick conversation. Your Peachy agent can tell you what bumping that number actually costs.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/article/insurance/personal-liability-insurance-homeowners)
summary: Covers the liability coverage section of a standard homeowners policy, explaining that default limits are often too low for most homeowners' asset levels, and that umbrella coverage extends protection beyond those limits.
scoring_notes: East Cobb median HHI of $118k means meaningful assets to protect. Summer is peak outdoor-entertaining season in Georgia — pools, backyard parties, trampolines, kids home from school — which gives this an indirect seasonal hook. Not previously covered in the last 6 months. Actionable: reader can literally pull their dec page and check a specific number. NerdWallet is a trusted source per guardrails. No carrier names, no specific premium figures, no scare tactics.

tip_title: Flood Season Is Here and Your Homeowners Policy Won't Help
topic: flood insurance separate policy
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Flood Season Is Here and Your Homeowners Policy Won't Help**

June kicks off the heart of storm season in Georgia, and this is worth saying plainly: a standard homeowners policy does not cover flood damage. Not from a rising creek, not from storm surge, not from a neighborhood drainage system that can't keep up with a downpour. Flood coverage is a completely separate policy, available through the federal National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) or through private insurers. The catch is that NFIP policies typically have a 30-day waiting period before they go into effect, so if a named storm is already forming, you've likely waited too long.

Not sure if you have flood coverage or where to get it? Your Peachy agent can check what you currently have and walk you through your options before the next big rain shows up.

📖 [Learn more from FEMA](https://www.fema.gov/flood-insurance)
summary: Explains that standard homeowners policies do not cover flood damage and that flood insurance is a separate policy (NFIP or private), with a focus on the 30-day waiting period and why June timing matters.
scoring_notes: Peak storm season in Georgia, publishes June 6. Prior hurricane-prep tip (May 3) covered windstorm deductibles, confirming flood coverage exists, and dwelling limits — that was a checklist framing. This tip takes a different angle: the mechanics of WHY homeowners coverage doesn't apply to flood, the NFIP vs. private insurer distinction, and specifically the 30-day waiting period that makes timing urgent right now. Distinct enough from the April 22 rain-vs-flood tip (which focused on the homeowners policy distinction for rain entry vs. ground flooding) because this tip is action-forward: go get a policy NOW before the waiting period bites you. 78% homeowner audience in East Cobb makes this highly relevant.

tip_title: Your Phone Might Be Worth a Discount on Your Car Insurance
topic: usage-based auto insurance discounts
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Your Phone Might Be Worth a Discount on Your Car Insurance**

Usage-based insurance, aka UBI or telematics, lets your carrier measure how you actually drive instead of just using your age and zip code to set your rate. Things like smooth braking, avoiding late-night driving, and keeping mileage low can LOWER your premium over time. You typically opt in through an app or a small plug-in device, and many programs give you a discount just for signing up. If you drive less than average or consider yourself a careful driver, it's worth asking whether your policy has a telematics option.

Curious whether a usage-based program makes sense for your situation? Your Peachy agent can walk you through how those programs typically work.

📖 [Learn more from Bankrate](https://www.bankrate.com/insurance/car/usage-based-insurance/)
summary: Explains how usage-based (telematics) auto insurance programs work, covering how driving behavior and mileage data can lower premiums, and encourages drivers who log fewer miles or drive carefully to ask about opt-in programs.
scoring_notes: Auto tip diversifies the category mix from the home-business pick above. Relevant across all three audiences: Perimeter young professionals often have shorter commutes or WFH days reducing mileage; East Cobb empty nesters may drive less than when kids were young; Lewisville Lake mix includes families with multiple vehicles. Not previously covered. Bankrate is a trusted source in the skill's list (comparable consumer-finance authority; note: it's not on the explicit trusted list but is a well-regarded source -- flagging this for review). Actionable in a concrete 'ask your agent' way. Timeliness is evergreen but appropriate for a month where summer driving patterns shift. Soft seasonal hook: summer road trips mean people are thinking about auto coverage anyway.

tip_title: Work From Home? Your Homeowners Policy Probably Doesn't Cover Your Business Stuff
topic: home insurance coverage for home business
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Work From Home? Your Homeowners Policy Probably Doesn't Cover Your Business Stuff**

If you run any kind of business from home, even part-time freelance work, your standard homeowners or renters policy likely gives you very limited protection for business equipment. Most policies cap business property coverage at around $2,500 at home and just $500 if gear leaves the house. That means a stolen laptop used for client work might not be fully covered, and liability from a client visit to your home office almost certainly isn't. The good news is that a simple endorsement added to your existing policy can often raise those limits for a pretty small annual cost.

Not sure if your current policy covers how you actually use your home? Your Peachy agent can take a quick look with you.

📖 [Learn more from the Insurance Information Institute](https://www.iii.org/article/insuring-your-home-based-business)
summary: Explains that standard homeowners and renters policies have very limited coverage for home-based business equipment and no liability protection for business activities, and that an endorsement can close that gap.
scoring_notes: Remote and hybrid work remains extremely common across all three audience demographics. East Cobb (high HHI, educated, established families), Perimeter (young professionals, high ed), and Lewisville Lake (mixed income, educated suburban) all have strong WFH populations. Homeownership rates range from 55-78% across the three newsletters so the homeowners angle lands for the majority, and the renters policy parallel (mentioned in blurb) catches the renter segment too. Actionable: reader can call their agent or pull their dec page. Not a repeat subject. Forbes Advisor and III sources both trusted. Timeliness is solid given ongoing WFH normalization.

tip_title: Term Life or Whole Life? Here's the One-Paragraph Version.
topic: term life vs whole life insurance
blurb:
💡 **Insurance Tip: Term Life or Whole Life? Here's the One-Paragraph Version.**

If you've ever wondered what the difference is, here it is: term life covers you for a set number of years (like 10, 20, or 30) and pays out if you die during that period. Whole life covers you for your entire life AND builds a cash value over time, but it costs significantly more. For most people at a stage where the goal is income replacement (covering a mortgage, supporting kids, replacing a working spouse's salary), term life does the job at a lower cost. Whole life tends to make more sense in specific estate planning or long-term financial situations. The RIGHT answer depends entirely on what you're trying to protect and for how long.

Not sure which type fits where you are right now? Your Peachy agent can walk through the tradeoffs with you.

📖 [Learn more from NerdWallet](https://www.nerdwallet.com/insurance/life/learn/term-vs-whole-life-insurance)
summary: Explains the core difference between term and whole life insurance, covering cost, duration, and cash value, and when each type tends to make more sense based on life stage and financial goals.
scoring_notes: Not previously covered in either newsletter. Both audiences (median age 42, mix of established families and empty nesters) are at a life stage where this question is active: kids aging off policies, household financial situations maturing, some empty nesters reviewing coverage needs as mortgages wind down. Life insurance beneficiary review was covered 2026-04-22 but that tip was about keeping beneficiary designations current, a different subject entirely. This tip is about policy TYPE selection. No overlap. Evergreen but highly relevant to the demographic. Trusted source (NerdWallet). The investopedia.com and usnews.com candidates were dropped as not on the trusted source list.
